title: X-ray Fluoroscopy, Hosp Schafer, San Lucas
description:
Hospitalito in Santiago needs an X-ray machine!

The objectives of this global grant are to purchase and install two greatly needed pieces of medical equipment into the Hospital in San Lucas Toliman, Guatemala. The first is a full body x-ray machine with CR digital developing or image acquisition technology. The second is a mini-C-arm portable fluoroscopy machine, primarily for use in the operating room but also for use in the ER and bedside.
narrative:
## GG1871794 — C-arm X-ray Fluoroscopy Unit for San Lucas Toliman Hospital
*Global Grant · 2018–2020 · San Lucas Toliman, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
The Rotary Club of Greater Bend (District 5110) partnered with the E-Club of Lake Atitlán (District 4250) to procure, ship, and install a refurbished Siemens Siremibil C-arm X-ray fluoroscopy unit at Hospital Obras Sociales de Monsignor Gregorio Schaffer in rural Guatemala. The unit provides real-time intraoperative imaging for surgical, orthopedic, and emergency procedures, dramatically improving surgical capability at this mission hospital. Surplus grant funds were ultimately used to upgrade the hospital's electrical wiring to support the new equipment.

---

### Community Served
The hospital serves a largely indigenous population of approximately 35,000 people in and around San Lucas Toliman, Atitlán region, Guatemala, providing low-cost or free healthcare including multiple international surgical mission trips each year.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $49,950 |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $22,125 |
| District 5110 DDF | $16,500 |
| Club cash contributions (all clubs) | $11,325 |

*Contributing clubs: Greater Bend ($9,075), Shasta Valley/Yreka ($1,000), Roseburg Morning ($1,000), E-Club of Lake Atitlán-Panajachel ($100); District 4250 cash ($150).*

---

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs:**
- Rotary Club of Greater Bend, District 5110 *(Oregon, USA — international sponsor)*
- E-Club of Lake Atitlán-Panajachel, District 4250 *(Guatemala — host sponsor)*
- Rotary Club of Shasta Valley (Yreka), District 5110 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Roseburg Morning, District 5110 *(Oregon, USA)*

**Equipment & Logistics Vendors:**
- Seattle Technologies — equipment supplier (Siemens C-arm, digital image capture, surge protector, crating, shipping, insurance)
- Kuehne Nagel, S.A. — shipping agent and customs broker
- Siemens Service — installation and staff training
- Guatemalan Department of Mines — X-ray licensing authority

---

### Key Personnel
- **A.J. Garis** — International primary contact, Rotary Club of Greater Bend; project originator and financial manager
- **William Boegel** — Host primary contact, E-Club of Lake Atitlán
- **Emilio Crespo Morales** & **Mayra Tobías** — Host committee secondary contacts, Lake Atitlán
- **Mark Miller** & **Gary Meyer** — International committee secondary contacts, Greater Bend
- **Daniel Xutj** — Hospital radiology technician; trained on unit; monitoring and evaluation lead
- **Dr. Tun**, **Julio Lopez**, and **Daniel Xep** — Local hospital staff involved in planning and ongoing oversight
- **Greg Fowler** — Club treasurer, Greater Bend; co-managed project finances

---

### Outcomes
- Refurbished Siemens Siremibil C-arm fluoroscopy unit procured, shipped, and installed at hospital surgical ward
- Unit operational by March 2019; used during a 10-day surgical mission by a team of 40 U.S. physicians
- More than 50 children received reconstructive surgeries (feet, ankles, legs, hips, hands, arms) during the first mission alone
- 75 operative X-rays performed (tracked at progress report stage); 45 medical/health professionals trained
- 750 people reported as directly benefiting from the project (final report)
- Guatemalan Department of Mines X-ray license obtained; Rotary customs waiver secured
- Hospital electrical wiring upgraded using $7,616.76 in surplus grant funds — directly supporting equipment operation
- Full $49,950 budget expended with zero unspent funds returned

---

### Project Story
This project came together after A.J. Garis of the Greater Bend club connected with E-Club of Lake Atitlán representatives at the 25th Annual Rotary Project Fair in Belize City, leading to a partnership that took nearly a year longer than originally planned to execute — the application targeted a June–September 2018 timeline, but equipment was not ordered until December 2018 and the unit was not delivered and installed until March 2019. The project encountered meaningful scope shifts along the way: the team negotiated a lower equipment price, switched from air freight to sea freight (reducing shipping costs), and training came in under budget — but equipment costs ultimately exceeded the budget line by over $2,000. Rather than return the resulting $7,617 surplus to the Foundation, the team identified a practical and directly related use: upgrading the hospital's aging electrical infrastructure to safely support the new machine, a decision reflected in the final report. The handoff was clean — the hospital owns the equipment outright, Siemens' regional presence in Central America ensures parts availability, and local staff (led by radiology technician Daniel Xutj) were trained for ongoing operations and future mentoring.

---

### Notes
- The project was initially conceived with air freight; actual shipping was completed by sea, reflecting pragmatic cost management in the field.
- The electrical upgrade (Cableado project) was an unplanned but well-documented addition, with supporting Spanish-language cost quotation submitted with the final report.
- The grant formally closed in 2020 following final report authorization by both sponsor clubs.

title: Healthcare in Patantic & Sanika-ya
description:
Improving the health of the indigenous people in Patanatic and San Luis, Guatemala
The main objective of this grant is to improve access to health for the indigenous families of Patanatic and San Luis, Sololá, Guatemala. This project will provide 3 interventions, the first will be a health education program implemented by children and youth who will act as health promoters. The second intervention will be disease prevention by offering diagnostic studies for cervical and breast cancer, and the third intervention will be primary medical care for mothers and diabetic and hypertensive patients in the community.
narrative:
## GG2352598 — Improving Health of Indigenous People in Patanatic and San Luis, Guatemala
*Global Grant · 2023–2026 · Patanatic and San Luis, Sololá, Guatemala*

### What We Did
This project delivered three integrated health interventions to indigenous communities near Lake Atitlán: training young community members as health promoters, providing cervical and breast cancer screening to women of childbearing age, and offering primary medical care (including chronic disease management) to mothers and patients with diabetes or hypertension. The grant funded medical consultations, diagnostic imaging, medications, educational materials, and technology for a year-long health promoter training curriculum. All services were delivered through the Patanatic Health and Education Center (CESP).

### Community Served
Residents of Patanatic and San Luis villages in the municipality of Panajachel, Sololá Department, Guatemala — an indigenous Maya community with severely limited access to preventive and curative health care, served by only one departmental hospital for more than 467,000 inhabitants.

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant | $49,886 USD |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $17,520 USD |
| District Designated Funds (DDF) — Districts 4250, 5710, 5680 | $21,900 USD |
| Club cash contributions (7 clubs) | $7,028 USD |
| Endowed/Directed Gift (MGB Guatemala Term Gift, District 4250) | $3,438 USD |

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs & Districts**
- Lake Atitlán Rotary Club, District 4250 — Host Club (Guatemala)
- Topeka South Rotary Club, District 5710 — International Sponsor (Kansas, USA)
- Leawood Rotary Club (Kansas, USA)
- Village West (Kansas City) Rotary Club (Kansas, USA)
- Manhattan Konza Rotary Club (Kansas, USA)
- Russell Rotary Club (Kansas, USA)
- Overland Park South Rotary Club (Kansas, USA)
- Rotary District 5710 (USA)
- Rotary District 5680 (USA)
- Rotary District 4250 (Guatemala)

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- Asociación Integral de Educación y Salud Patanatic (AIESP) — primary cooperating organization; received and managed funds, executed programs, maintained financial records
- Centro Educativo y de Salud Patanatic (CESP) — facility where services were delivered
- Instituto Nacional de Cancerología de Guatemala (INCAN) — Pap smear processing
- APROFAM / IMESSA — breast ultrasound and mammography services
- Gophilanthropic Foundation — long-term sustainability funding partner

### Key Personnel
- **Faron Barr** — International Primary Contact, Topeka South RC, District 5710
- **Duncan Aitken** — Host Primary Contact, Lake Atitlán RC, District 4250
- **Dr. David García** — Director, AIESP; native community physician whose education was funded by Rotarians; clinical and program leader on the ground
- **Adela De Tecún** — Legal Representative, AIESP; local community leader for 10+ years; oversaw contracts and financial compliance
- **Mark Weinstein & Armand Boissy** — Host Committee Secondary Contacts, Lake Atitlán RC
- **Angela Dailey & Jessica Lehnherr** — International Committee Secondary Contacts, Topeka South RC

### Outcomes
- **15–18 young health promoters** trained over 10 months in an 8-module curriculum (hygiene, nutrition, disease prevention, first aid, glucose monitoring); graduates committed to training 20 additional community members each
- **291–345 students** in Patanatic and San Luis educational centers trained on preventive health, hand washing, and healthy lifestyles
- **100 women** received gynecological consultations, Pap smears, and cervical cancer education; 87 tests completed by progress report, with 30 abnormalities identified on Pap smear and 5 on physical exam — all placed in follow-up
- **100 women** received breast cancer screening (ultrasound and/or mammography); 16 ultrasound abnormalities and 5 mammography abnormalities identified — all placed in follow-up
- **420 mothers** received up to 3 primary care consultations per year; 45 found with uncontrolled chronic disease, 32 with untreated elective surgical conditions, 5 with vector-borne disease, 5 with malnutrition — all in follow-up
- **35–77 patients** with diabetes and/or hypertension received specialized consultations, nutrition counseling, weekly glucometry, home visits, and essential medications; complications identified in 8 (diabetes) and 3 (hypertension) patients, all in follow-up
- **1,477 total direct beneficiaries** reported in the final report
- All 18 planned project activities completed; medical equipment and supplies transferred to CESP as community property

### Project Story
The project launched on schedule in early 2024 and executed all three interventions largely as planned, with the health promoter curriculum, student training, and chronic disease programs running concurrently throughout the year. A notable budget variance emerged in the progress report — only Q. 340,710 of the projected Q. 376,625 had been received at that stage, creating a temporary shortfall — but by the final report, total expenditures of Q. 388,283 slightly exceeded the original budget (by ~Q. 11,658), with personnel costs running over and training costs coming in under, reflecting extended service delivery into early 2025 and again through late 2025. The project's timeline extended well beyond the original end date of June 2024, with medical services and follow-up continuing through at least October 2025, reflecting both the depth of need uncovered (including unexpected disease burden) and the team's commitment to seeing patients through to resolution. The most significant lasting impact is structural: the trained health promoters, the strengthened CESP facility, and AIESP's financial management capacity all remain in the community, with the Gophilanthropic Foundation providing ongoing operational support — making this a genuine capacity-building success rather than a one-time campaign.

### Notes
- This grant builds on more than a decade of prior Rotary investment in Patanatic, including the construction and equipping of the CESP building and the Rotary-funded medical education of Dr. David García, a native community physician.
- The broad multi-club, multi-district funding coalition (seven clubs across three U.S. districts plus the Guatemala host district) reflects the maturity of the Topeka South–Lake Atitlán partnership.
- The cancer screening program identified a significant burden of undiagnosed abnormalities: collectively, 35 women had concerning findings on cervical or breast examination, underscoring the critical gap the project filled.

title: Amigos de Santa Cruz 1 / Childhood education and basic business skills
description:
Amigos de Santa Cruz Economic Development Project

Childhood education and basic business skills have been all but unknown in this indigenous community. The Rotary Foundation’s Global Grant is now contributing to educating a generation for the first time. Both early education and adult vocational training – particularly for women – are providing individuals with knowledge and skills to continue their education, gain employment and sustain healthier homes and families. This grant is specifically working to prepare 50 indigenous women to establish, operate, build and ideally, to expand textile and culinary enterprises.
narrative:
## GG2346063 — Amigos de Santa Cruz Economic Development Project
*Global Grant · 2023–2024 · Santa Cruz La Laguna, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project provided comprehensive economic development training to approximately 50 Indigenous Maya women in the Santa Cruz La Laguna community, preparing them to launch or expand small businesses. Training covered personal empowerment and self-esteem, financial literacy and business administration, and technical skills in six business areas: mushroom cultivation, mushroom patties, organic eggs, honey production, chocolate making, and soaps and shampoos. Upon graduation, each woman received the equipment and supplies needed to establish her business.

### Community Served
The 9,000 Indigenous Kaq'chikel Maya residents of Santa Cruz La Laguna, Sololá — one of Guatemala's poorest departments (64% poverty rate, HDI of 0.48) — where geographic isolation, high illiteracy (37%), COVID-19 economic devastation, and lack of women's economic opportunity created urgent need.

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $40,600 USD (GTQ 312,600) |
| World Fund (Rotary Foundation) | $11,600 USD |
| Club cash contribution (Walkerton RC) | $14,500 USD |
| District Designated Fund (District 6330) | $14,500 USD |
| Small overage covered by RC Lake Atitlan donation | ~$795 USD equivalent |

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs:**
- Rotary Club of Walkerton, District 6330 *(international sponsor — Ontario, Canada)*
- Rotary e-Club of Lake Atitlan–Panajachel, District 4250 *(host sponsor — Guatemala)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations:**
- **Amigos de Santa Cruz** (cooperating organization) — experienced local NGO operating in the Santa Cruz region since 1998, with 97% Guatemalan staff (85% from Santa Cruz itself); responsible for program delivery, trainer procurement, expense reporting, equipment distribution, and three-year post-project monitoring.

### Key Personnel
- **Donald Moore** — Walkerton RC, international primary contact; prepared grant application and final reports
- **Armand Boissy** — Lake Atitlan RC, host primary contact and club treasurer; managed all financial disbursements and bank records
- **Jessie Cohn** — Executive Director, Amigos de Santa Cruz (since 2017); monitoring and evaluation lead
- **David Griggs & Mark Weinstein** — Lake Atitlan RC, host committee secondary contacts
- **Bonnelyne Noble (Park) & Adam Hofmann** — Walkerton RC, international committee secondary contacts
- **Julio Grazioso** (District 4250 DRFC) and **David Elliott** (District 6330 DRFC) — district authorizations

### Outcomes
- **50 women** trained across all program streams (met original target)
- **8 preparation workshops** completed (self-esteem, rights, eco-feminism, literacy introduction)
- **10+ finance/business workshops** delivered covering sustainability, market analysis, finance administration, and marketing
- **Technical training** delivered in 5 of 6 planned business areas: mushroom patties, honey, organic eggs, mushroom cultivation, and chocolate making *(soaps/shampoos not confirmed in reports)*
- **8–9 businesses supported** at project close (up from 6 at mid-point)
- **38–42 jobs created** and sustained (slightly below the 50-woman peak, reflecting some attrition)
- **0 reported incidents of food insecurity** among participating families
- **56 children** of participating women tracked for school completion rates
- Post-graduation equipment and supplies delivered to all graduating women
- Amigos de Santa Cruz committed to **3 additional years** of monitoring and support
- Amigos continued delivering **8 further workshops** (Women's Business Development, Production, Business Administration) through June 2025

### Project Story
The project launched in May 2023 — about three months later than the February 2023 application date — and ran largely on plan through its core training period ending June 2024, with Amigos de Santa Cruz continuing support activities through June 2025. The budget overran slightly: actual expenditures reached GTQ 318,728 against a budget of GTQ 312,600, driven primarily by supplies costs exceeding budget by nearly GTQ 67,000 (offset by savings in equipment, travel, and operations); the Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan made a direct donation to cover the shortfall. Participant retention dipped from 50 at peak to 38–42 at project close, a real but modest attrition for a 13-month program serving women with significant life pressures. The project's most meaningful legacy is its sustainability design: Amigos de Santa Cruz — whose deep community roots and prior 80% employment-rate track record with culinary graduates gave confidence — remained engaged as a continuing support partner, and the women themselves own all purchased equipment and assets. No funds were returned to the Foundation.

### Notes
- The project's supply budget significantly underestimated ongoing consumable costs (feed, mushroom seed, hay, chemicals) once live businesses were operating — a lesson for similar agricultural micro-enterprise grants.
- Chocolate making training was planned but does not appear prominently in expense records; soaps and shampoos similarly unconfirmed — both may have been deprioritized in favour of higher-success-rate businesses (mushroom cultivation and organic eggs) per Amigos' established methodology.
- The grant application noted that interest in irregular northward migration was growing exponentially in the community — this project was explicitly framed as an economic alternative.

title: Water & Sanitation in Santa Lucia Utatlan and San Andreas
description:
The two villages we will be working in are Chuitzam, Santa Lucía Utatlan (population 682) and Chuti Estancia,
San Andreas Semetabaj (population 1520). Both of these villages have a severe potable water shortage and they
have to walk a long distance to carry water at this time.
narrative:
## GG2091778 — Water and Sanitation for Guatemalan Highland Villages
*Global Grant · 2020–2025 · Sololá Department, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project designed and built complete potable water systems — including spring catchment tanks, wells, pumps, distribution piping, and water meters — for two under-served villages in the Lake Atitlan watershed. Sanitation infrastructure (latrines, septic systems, and a biodigester-based wastewater treatment system) was added alongside community hygiene education and governance training. The goal was not only physical infrastructure but lasting self-sufficiency: communities signed covenants, established water committees, and began collecting usage tariffs to fund ongoing maintenance.

---

### Community Served
Two villages in the department of Sololá, Guatemala — Chuitzam (Santa Lucía Utatlan, pop. ~682) and Chuti Estancia (San Andreas Semetabaj, pop. ~1,520) — both of which lacked accessible potable water, requiring residents to carry water over a kilometer on foot.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $161,100 USD |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $67,700 |
| District Designated Funds (DDF) | $42,000 |
| Club cash contributions | $51,400 |
| Local currency budget (GTQ) | 1,208,243 |
| Total final expenditures (GTQ) | 1,239,873 (slight over-run, fully spent) |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs & Districts:**
- Northfield Rotary Club, District 5960 *(International sponsor — North America)*
- Lake Atitlan Rotary Club, District 4250 *(Host sponsor — Guatemala)*
- Glen Ellen-Kenwood RC; Petaluma RC; Petaluma Valley RC; Annapolis RC; Watsonville RC; Bluffton RC; Burnsville RC; Red Wing RC; Edina RC *(contributing clubs — North America)*
- Districts 5130, 5950, 5960, 7620 *(DDF contributors)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations:**
- Asociación Pro Agua del Pueblo (ADP), Quetzaltenango — primary implementing partner (engineering, construction, training, community organizing)
- Mil Milagros, Sololá — hygiene and health education in schools
- Municipalities of Santa Lucía Utatlan and San Andreas Semetabaj — co-signatories, donated materials
- Community COCODEs (local governance councils) — signed covenants, operate water systems

---

### Key Personnel
- **Todd Thompson** — International primary contact, Northfield RC, District 5960; PhD Chemist; volunteer traveler; water quality and chlorination consultant; financial co-manager throughout the project
- **Terrence Wakely** — Host primary contact, Lake Atitlan RC, District 4250
- **Mayra Tobías** — Host committee secondary contact, Lake Atitlan RC
- **Armand Boissy** — Host committee secondary contact, Lake Atitlan RC; signed legal agreement
- **Charles Cogan & Vicki Dilley** — International committee, Northfield RC
- **Joe Wakely** — Financial co-manager (later reports)
- **J. Kyle Haugen** — DRFC authorization, District 5960
- **Marcelo Coyi** — DRFC authorization, District 4250

---

### Outcomes
- **2,900 people** gained access to improved drinking water across both villages (final report)
- **155 homes** in Chuitzam and **395 homes** in Chuti Estancia connected to new water systems with individual water meters
- **1,200 people** gained access to improved sanitation facilities, including a biodigester wastewater treatment system serving ~10 homes (completed 2024)
- **2 functioning community governance committees** (COCODEs) established with water tariff structures in place and actively collecting fees
- **22 individuals trained** in technical maintenance, system operation, and hygiene
- Community covenants signed committing residents to pay water bills, provide unskilled labor, and attend annual certifications
- Hygiene education delivered through schools and community meetings (led by Mil Milagros and ADP)

---

### Project Story
This project was ambitious, collaborative, and ultimately successful — but it took roughly five years instead of the planned three. The Chuitzam system was completed relatively on schedule (by early 2021), delivering clean water to 158 families with governance and tariffs in place; a dedication ceremony was held. The Chuti Estancia system proved far more complex: electrical infrastructure for the pump was a persistent bottleneck, the COVID pandemic complicated travel and oversight, and material costs substantially exceeded the original budget (Equipment spending ran more than double the estimate at 819,000 GTQ vs. a budget of 338,000 GTQ), offset by significantly lower-than-expected labor costs. The team managed this honestly — the second progress report acknowledged the variance and the reason — and the project remained fully funded without returning money to the Foundation. A final small wastewater treatment biodigester system, awaiting municipal matching funds as late as early 2024, was completed by late 2024 and reported in the accepted final report submitted April 2025. The lasting handoff is strong: both communities own their infrastructure, operate water committees, collect tariffs, and have been trained in maintenance — exactly the sustainability model the project set out to achieve.

---

### Notes
- This was explicitly labeled **Phase 2**, indicating prior Rotary water/sanitation work in the same region.
- The project also contributes to **Lake Atitlan watershed protection**, an environmental concern of regional significance.
- Todd Thompson lived in Guatemala 2013–2015 and brought deep local knowledge to the international sponsor role — an unusual asset in cross-border Rotary projects.

title: El Potrero, WASH
description:
The community of El Potrero is part of a village called El Tablón which is in the northern part of the
municipality and department of Sololá. The Mayan language spoken by the people is Kakchiquel. This project
will increase awareness of the benefits of clean water, sanitation, and hygiene. It will also enhance and
strengthen the access and capacity of safe drinking water. One component will be interactive education
programs that will enhance the communities’ knowledge of the benefits of safe water, proper sanitation, and
good hygiene. A second component will be assistance in development a new safe water source for the
community and the training and education in the maintenance and governance of the system for a sustainable
future. A last component will be the installation of latrines. The beneficiaries are the families of El Potrero. El
Potrero is a hamlet in the village of El Tablón, in the municipality and department of Sololá, Guatemala.
narrative:
## GG2125241 — WASH Infrastructure for El Potrero, Guatemala
*Global Grant · 2022–2024 · Casarío El Potrero, Aldea El Tablón, Department of Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project aimed to deliver a comprehensive water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) solution to a small Kakchiquel Mayan community in rural Guatemala. The plan included constructing a new water catchment tank and distribution network, installing ventilated pit latrines, and providing hygiene education and governance training to ensure long-term sustainability. Ultimately, the water infrastructure components were completed, but the sanitation, training, and home-connection phases were cancelled when the community declined further outside assistance.

---

### Community Served
The approximately 350 residents of El Potrero (Casarío El Potrero, Aldea El Tablón, Sololá, Guatemala) — a Kakchiquel Mayan hamlet with no reliable safe drinking water source, inadequate sanitation, and limited hygiene education.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $50,342 USD |
| Rotary Foundation World Fund match | $16,818 USD |
| District 5440 DDF | $15,000 USD |
| District 6440 DDF | $6,024 USD |
| Fort Collins Rotary Club (cash) | $7,500 USD |
| Glenview-Sunrise Rotary Club (cash) | $5,000 USD |
| **Total actually spent** | ~$16,235 USD (GTQ 126,784) |
| **Returned to Foundation** | $34,107 USD |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Fort Collins, District 5440 *(International sponsor — North American)*
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan, District 4250 *(Host sponsor — Guatemala)*
- Rotary Club of Glenview-Sunrise, District 6440 *(Financial contributor — North American)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Engineers without Borders–USA Guatemala (EWB)** — Registered Guatemalan NGO; designed and managed construction of the water distribution system and tank; provided governance and maintenance training
- **Asociación Pro Agua del Pueblo (ADP)** — 40+ years of WASH experience in Guatemala; contracted for latrine construction and hygiene training (did not ultimately execute due to project cancellation)
- **Municipalidad de Sololá** — Local municipal government; provided environmental permits, equipment, and security coordination
- **COCODE** (Consejos Comunitarios de Desarrollo Urbano y Rural) — Community governance body; intended long-term operator of the water system

---

### Key Personnel
- **John Trone** — Fort Collins Rotary Club; International Primary Contact; financial manager
- **Shad Qudsi** — Lake Atitlan Rotary Club; Host Primary Contact
- **Duncan Aitken** — Lake Atitlan RC; Host Secondary Contact; co-signed legal agreement
- **Terrence Wakely** — Lake Atitlan RC; Host Secondary Contact
- **Gloria Gleave** — Fort Collins RC; International Secondary Contact
- **Richard Fisher** — Fort Collins RC; International Secondary Contact
- **Charles Rutenberg** — Fort Collins Breakfast RC; DRFC Chair, District 5440
- **Marcelo Coyi** — Belize RC; DRFC Chair, District 4250
- **Steve Kane** — Fort Collins RC Treasurer; co-approved fund transfers
- **Michael Rosen** — Lake Atitlan RC; Sponsor authorization signatory
- **Randall Kurtz** — Fort Collins RC; Sponsor authorization signatory

---

### Outcomes
- ✅ **Water distribution tank** constructed (July–August 2023; second phase completed July–August 2024)
- ✅ **2,408 linear meters of PVC pipe** (2" and 1" diameter) installed for the distribution network (completed September 2023)
- ✅ **Governance and maintenance training** partially delivered (GTQ 10,218 expended)
- ✅ **~350 people** reported as direct beneficiaries in the final report
- ❌ **Spring capture system** — cancelled
- ❌ **Home connections** — cancelled
- ❌ **Latrine construction** (15 ventilated pit latrines planned) — cancelled; ADP did not proceed
- ❌ **Hygiene and sanitation training** — cancelled
- ❌ **Signage** — never installed
- ⚠️ All formal impact metrics (people with improved water access, governance committee, tariff structure, individuals trained) reported as **0** in the final report, despite 350 beneficiaries being claimed

---

### Project Story
This project began with strong community engagement and a well-documented plan, but it was plagued by delays from the outset — construction on the water catchment tank did not begin until July 2023, nearly a year after the original project end date of December 2022. The water infrastructure work (tank and distribution lines) was ultimately completed through Engineers without Borders Guatemala, representing a genuine physical achievement for the community. However, at some point during implementation, the community of El Potrero indicated it no longer wanted outside assistance, leading to the cancellation of five of the seven planned activities — including latrines, home connections, spring capture, hygiene training, and governance training. The final report is notably inconsistent: 350 beneficiaries are claimed, yet all formal impact metrics are recorded as zero, suggesting either a reporting error or that the completed infrastructure had not yet been verified as operational and accessible. Approximately $34,107 — more than two-thirds of the total grant — was returned unused to the Rotary Foundation, making this a significantly incomplete project relative to its original scope.

---

### Notes
- The community's decision to decline further outside assistance mid-project is an unusual and important lesson for future grants: sustained community buy-in through implementation (not just planning) is critical.
- The discrepancy between "350 beneficiaries" and "0" on all impact measures in the final report was not explained and warrants follow-up if future work in this community is considered.
- A small amount of currency exchange income ($299 USD) was generated during fund transfers between the US and Guatemala.
- The project was originally scoped for completion by December 31, 2022; final reporting was not submitted until March 2024 — approximately 15 months late.

title: Stoves in San Lucas
description: Air pollution is a major global environmental health problem and contributes to over 7 million deaths annually. Poor indoor air quality also significantly impacts morbidity in mothers and childhood illnesses in Central American countries like Guatemala. We seek to install properly vented energy efficient stoves to replace the open-pit indoor cooking stoves used by indigenous Guatemalan families around Lake Aititlan. This solution offers improved indoor air quality for mothers and their children by reducing smoke inhalation effects, burn hazards, and less wood consumption/use. We anticipate 160+ family members in the communities of San Lucas Toliman, Guatemala will benefit in addition to local contractors for supporting the building resources and installation/maintenance needs of the stove systems.
narrative:
## GG1873788 — Vented Cooking Stoves for Indigenous Guatemalan Families
*Global Grant · 2019–2024 · San Lucas Toliman, Solola Region, Guatemala (Lake Atitlan)*

---

### What We Did
This project replaced open-pit indoor cooking stoves with properly vented, fuel-efficient wood-burning stoves in indigenous Guatemalan homes around Lake Atitlan. The goal was to reduce smoke and carbon monoxide exposure, lower burn hazards for children, and decrease wood consumption. Alongside stove installation, the project trained community health workers in respiratory health assessment and delivered environmental health literacy education to families, teachers, and local health promoters across five communities.

---

### Community Served
Indigenous families in San Lucas Toliman and surrounding villages in the Solola region of Guatemala — a community of approximately 30,000 people served by a single physician, where ~70% of female clinic patients presented with respiratory problems linked to indoor smoke exposure.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount (USD) |
|------|-------------|
| Total grant budget | $55,823 |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $25,373 |
| District 5960 DDF contributions | $20,300 |
| Club cash contributions (Rochester, Rochester Risers, Greater Rochester) | $10,150 |
| Other sources | Not stated |

---

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs:**
- Rochester Rotary Club, District 5960 (Minnesota, USA) — International sponsor
- Rochester Risers Rotary Club, District 5960 (Minnesota, USA)
- Greater Rochester Rotary Club, District 5960 (Minnesota, USA)
- Lake Atitlan Rotary Club (e-Club), District 4250 — Host sponsor
- Real de Minas-Tegucigalpa Rotary Club, District 4250

**NGOs & Local Organizations:**
- Friends of St. Lucas Toliman (Eagan, MN, USA) — Coordinated vendors, logistics, and stove installation oversight; key field partner throughout the project
- Rochester Community and Technical College (RCTC), Minnesota — Provided educator volunteers and developed educational materials
- San Lucas Mission / San Lucas Toliman District Hospital — Local medical and community infrastructure

---

### Key Personnel
- **Ashok Patel** (Rochester Rotary, D5960) — International primary contact; Mayo Clinic pulmonologist; project lead
- **Duncan Aitken** (Lake Atitlan Rotary, D4250) — Host primary contact
- **Dr. Raphael Tun** — Medical Director, San Lucas Toliman District Hospital; monitoring and evaluation lead
- **Lillyam Arroyave** — Spanish instructor, RCTC; sociolinguistic and educational lead; in-country liaison
- **Don Baldus** — Emeritus RCTC staff; volunteer mentor and educator
- **Bill Peterson** — Friends of St. Lucas Toliman coordinator; critical field operations lead
- **Ramiro Coche** — Local stove repair coordinator
- **Catherine Patel** & **Brad Fischer** — Volunteer travelers; educational and marketing materials
- **David Griggs** & **Jack Bower** — Lake Atitlan club, host secondary contacts
- **Alison Good** & **Phil Karsell** — Rochester club, international secondary contacts

---

### Outcomes
- **208 vented stoves installed** across multiple villages (exceeding the original goal of 160, due to favorable material pricing)
- **~1,208 direct beneficiaries** reported in the final report, with an estimated 1,000+ family members served and an additional ~500 educators and health promoters reached
- **Old open-pit stoves removed** and disposed of as part of the installation process
- **Community health workers trained** in basic lung health assessment, spirometry use, pulse oximetry, and air quality monitoring; 49 health/medical personnel reached
- **Environmental health literacy curriculum** developed in English and Spanish, delivered to health promoters, school teachers, and families across 5 communities
- **3 limited-term scholarships** awarded to local students to sustain eco-literacy promotion
- **Monthly follow-up visits** conducted by community health workers for stove condition and respiratory health assessment over the post-installation period
- **Partnerships reinforced** between Rochester/D5960 Rotarians, Friends of St. Lucas, Dr. Tun's medical team, RCTC, and local community elders and educators

---

### Project Story
This project launched in June 2019 with strong community roots — built on a 2015–2017 pilot that installed 25 stoves — but was significantly disrupted by the COVID-19 pandemic, which forced cancellation of the planned July 2020 Rotary site visit and shifted all coordination to phone, Zoom, and email. Despite this, in-country partners (particularly Bill Peterson at Friends of St. Lucas and Dr. Tun's team) continued stove construction steadily, completing 75 stoves by early 2021 and all 160 originally planned by late 2021, with an additional 48 stoves installed by mid-2023 using favorable budget savings. The international team finally conducted an in-person site visit in May 2023, delivering educational sessions and recognizing local health promoters, and a follow-up visit was completed by December 2023. Budget variances were significant across most categories — training expenditures more than doubled the original allocation as the educational component expanded, while early categories like accommodations and monitoring were underspent due to COVID delays — but the full budget was expended with no funds returned to the Foundation. The project concluded with a robust handoff: local vendors, community health workers, and newly scholarshipped student promoters are positioned to sustain stove maintenance and eco-literacy training independently, representing a genuine "training the trainers" legacy aligned with the project's founding vision.

---

### Notes
- The project originally planned formal spirometry and pulmonary function testing as a monitoring component; this was largely displaced by the shift to remote operations during COVID and replaced with qualitative health assessments and focus groups.
- Financial management faced recurring challenges across the project's five-year span, including Rotary leadership transitions, club administrator turnover, and a mid-project change of bank accounts (US Bank to F&M Bank), all of which delayed reimbursements to Guatemalan partners.
- The project was cited in the final report as a model for Rotary-medical partnerships emphasizing environmental health literacy and "One Health" education frameworks.

title: Maternal Health, Tecpan Chimaltenango
description: The goal of our project is implement a comprehensive, 1000 Days-focused package of interventions in a rural, indigenous Maya community with very high rates of poverty, chronic malnutrition, and lack of access to health care. This program will be conducted in partnership with Maya Health Alliance, a local Guatemalan organization which is a recognized leader in the 1000 Days paradigm in Guatemala and an innovator in the prevention and treatment of stunting in rural Guatemala. We anticipate that this program will serve as a model for other organizations working to implement effective 1000 Days-focused programs in Guatemala.
narrative:
## GG1870757 — Maternal & Child Nutrition: Guatemala 1,000 Days Initiative
*Global Grant · 2018–2021 · Tecpán, Chimaltenango, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project delivered a comprehensive "1,000 Days" nutrition intervention to 140 families in a remote, indigenous Kaqchikel-Maya farming village, targeting children under two years of age and their caregivers. Community health workers made home visits over six months providing micronutrient and food supplementation, breastfeeding and complementary feeding education, and early child development coaching. A complementary home garden was built for each enrolled family to improve long-term household food security.

---

### Community Served
A remote farming village of approximately 230 Kaqchikel-Maya speaking families in the department of Chimaltenango, Guatemala — one of the highest-malnutrition, highest-maternal-mortality communities in the region due to extreme geographic isolation and poverty.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant | $95,600 USD |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $39,525 |
| District 5960 DDF | $22,975 |
| Club cash contributions (multiple clubs) | $18,100 |
| Non-Rotarian contributions (Family Humanitarian Experience NGO) | $15,000 |
| Unused funds returned to Foundation | $4,485 |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rochester Risers Rotary Club — District 5960 *(International sponsor, Minnesota, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Rochester — District 5960 *(North America, contributing club)*
- Greater Rochester Rotary — District 5960 *(North America, contributing club)*
- E-Club of Lake Atitlán-Panajachel — District 4250 *(Host sponsor, Guatemala)*
- Additional contributing clubs: Hayward Area, New Brighton/Mounds View, Lakeville, Stillwater Sunrise, Woodbury, Red Wing, River Falls, Vail (all District 5960, North America)

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Maya Health Alliance (Wuqu' Kawoq)** — Primary implementing partner; hired/trained field staff, delivered all interventions, managed in-country finances
- **Cultiva International** — Guatemala-based NGO; provided horticultural expertise for home garden design
- **Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics** (Chicago, USA) — Provided technical support, monitoring/evaluation tools, and embedded an Applied Nutrition Fellow in-country (through early 2020)
- **Family Humanitarian Experience** — NGO contributing $15,000 in matched non-Rotarian funds

---

### Key Personnel
- **Joanne Rosener** — International primary contact, Rochester Risers; Masters in nutrition; led financial oversight from US side
- **Duncan Aitken** — Host primary contact, Lake Atitlán Club
- **Dr. Peter Rohloff** — Chief Medical Officer, Maya Health Alliance; Harvard Medical School faculty; oversaw all monitoring and evaluation
- **Anne Kraemer Diaz** — Maya Health Alliance Guatemala; managed in-country finances and invoicing
- **Jennifer Brewer, MA, RDN** — Project coordinator/volunteer traveler; coordinated inter-organizational relationships; traveled to Guatemala August 2019
- **Michelle Majerus** — Rochester Risers; managed US-side finances through 2020, then handed off to Joanne Rosener
- **Adriana Sánchez** — Joined as host club secondary contact; became primary host contact after Will Boegel departed the club

---

### Outcomes
- **140 families enrolled**; 140 baseline surveys completed
- **All 140 children** received the full 6-month nutrition-specific intervention package (micronutrients, food supplementation, breastfeeding/complementary feeding education)
- **79 families** completed 6-month follow-up surveys; **14 families** completed 12-month follow-ups before COVID halted fieldwork
- **140 home gardens implemented** across all enrolled families (65 built pre-COVID; remaining ~75 completed summer–fall 2021)
- Training materials for community health workers and caregiver home-visit curriculum fully developed and delivered
- Secondary outcomes tracked: dietary diversity of pregnant/lactating women (73 surveyed), family-level food security (30 surveyed), children experiencing stunting (100 tested), and child developmental milestones (98 observed)
- **200 people** reported as direct beneficiaries
- Project completed; $4,485 in unused funds returned to the Rotary Foundation

---

### Project Story
This project launched on schedule in mid-2018 and hit its stride quickly: training materials were completed by January 2019, all 140 children were enrolled by December 2019, and the first half of home gardens were built and producing by early 2020. Then COVID-19 struck Guatemala in early 2020, halting all fieldwork entirely — the remaining ~75 gardens could not be built, and physical measurements of children became impossible. The team adapted by switching to telephone-based follow-up interviews to preserve data collection, but the project's originally planned 24-month timeline stretched to over three years. After a year-long pause, fieldwork resumed in summer 2021 with newly hired and re-trained staff, and the final gardens were completed in October 2021, fully closing out the project. The budget saw meaningful category-level shifts — equipment and personnel ran over budget while supplies and monitoring/evaluation came in significantly under — but the total came in close to plan with a small return to the Foundation. Maya Health Alliance retained ownership of all equipment including the project truck and continues the 1,000 Days program independently in the community.

---

### Notes
- The project truck (2018 Toyota, ~$24,000 USD) was the single largest budget item and was specifically justified by the absence of reliable bus service on remote mountain roads; it remains with Maya Health Alliance for ongoing use.
- The monitoring/evaluation budget line ($8,200 budgeted) went entirely unspent — evaluation was absorbed into personnel and partner activities rather than billed separately.
- The Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics fellowship ended with COVID; no Academy staff remained on project after mid-2020, with Maya Health Alliance absorbing that role.
- The project was intended as a replicable model for 1,000 Days programming in rural Guatemala; Maya Health Alliance has indicated plans to expand the approach to other communities including Sololá.

description: This global grant was a partnership between Rotary Club of Asheville, NC in District 7670, the Rotary E-Club of Lake Atitlán in Panajachel, Guatemala, and PEG Partners Inc., a non-profit based in Black Mountain, NC and founded by Rotary Peace Fellow David LaMotte. narrative:
## GG1985748 — Simple School in Tzanchaj, Guatemala
*Global Grant · 2020–2022 · Tzanchaj, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project funded the design and construction of a new three-classroom school building for the Escuela Comunitaria David LaMotte, a free bilingual preschool and kindergarten serving Mayan children in rural Guatemala. The school had been operating for fourteen years but lost its physical space when a land lease expired; grant funds built a permanent structure on land the school had since purchased. The completed facility includes gender-separated bathrooms, hand-washing stations, a library, administrative office, kitchen, water filtration system, playground, and landscaping — all meeting Guatemalan Ministry of Education standards.

### Community Served
The school serves approximately 60–75 Mayan children (ages 3–5) in the village of Tzanchaj, near Santiago Atitlán, Sololá, where most families are illiterate, 80% do not speak Spanish, and many cannot afford even basic public school costs such as pencils or uniforms.

### Budget
| Item | Amount (USD) |
|------|-------------|
| Total grant budget | $117,839 |
| Rotary Foundation World Fund match | $47,659 |
| District Designated Funds (District 7670) | $25,137 |
| Cash contributions — Rotary clubs | $45,043 |

*Club cash contributors: Rotary Club of Asheville ($27,060), Asheville Breakfast ($5,000), Hickory ($4,000), Madison County ($2,500), Denver/Lake Norman ($2,883), Hendersonville-Four Seasons ($2,000), Black Mountain ($1,000), Asheville-Biltmore ($600).*

---

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Asheville, District 7670 — international sponsor (North Carolina, USA)
- Rotary E-Club of Lake Atitlán–Panajachel (RECLA), District 4250 — host sponsor (Guatemala)
- Rotary Club of Asheville Breakfast, District 7670 (North Carolina, USA)
- Multiple District 7670 clubs as financial contributors (Black Mountain, Hickory, Madison County, Hendersonville-Four Seasons, Denver/Lake Norman, Asheville-Biltmore)

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **PEG Partners, Inc.** (Black Mountain, NC) — founding NGO partner with a 14-year relationship with the school; provided grant-writing expertise, translation, teacher training support, procurement assistance, and ongoing financial oversight. Founded by Rotary Peace Fellow David LaMotte.
- **Escuela Comunitaria David LaMotte / Asociación** — the incorporated Guatemalan school entity that owns the land and building.
- **Puerta Abierta** (Santiago, Guatemala) — Montessori-model school providing continuing teacher education.
- **Mayanza and BEAMS** — US-based public health NGOs providing health screenings and dental services to school children.

---

### Key Personnel
- **William Rawlings** — Rotary Club of Asheville, international primary contact; led application writing, liaison with Rotary International
- **Duncan Aitken** — RECLA, host primary contact; 30-year Panajachel resident, fluent in Spanish; oversaw all on-site construction monitoring and fund disbursement
- **Mark Mains** — RECLA (later departed club); master educator with Guatemala teaching experience; led teacher training program design
- **Armand Boissy** — RECLA; extensive local construction experience; assisted with construction oversight
- **John Van Lente** — RECLA president; secondary contact
- **Nancy Brooks** — Rotary Club of Asheville; financial auditor for both the district and club
- **Jim Efland** — Rotary Club of Asheville Breakfast (PDG); construction and engineering guidance; participated in initial needs assessment
- **Sharon LeDuc** — Rotary Club of Asheville; progress report writing and club communications
- **Sarah Robinson-Bryan** — Director of PEG Partners; ongoing liaison and project oversight
- **David LaMotte** — Founder of PEG Partners and Rotary Peace Fellow Class VII; inspiration for the project
- **Ceilidh Moulden** — Canadian accountant in Panajachel; hired to maintain financial records in both GTQ and USD
- **Melchora Reanda Rujuch** — School Director; managed procurement of bids, permits, land documents, and coordinated with teachers on building design
- **Mario Pablo Benito** — School groundskeeper and guardian; responsible for ongoing building maintenance

---

### Outcomes
- ✅ Three-classroom school building fully constructed and inaugurated November 2021
- ✅ 75+ children enrolled (ages 3–5, preschool and kindergarten); 15 new students added
- ✅ 5 teachers received bilingual and early childhood education training plus hygiene education (completed January–March 2022)
- ✅ Gender-separated bathrooms, hand-washing stations, and water filtration system installed
- ✅ Library, administrative office, and kitchen fitted in existing structures on the property
- ✅ Playground constructed and landscaping completed
- ✅ Cypress Rotary signage installed
- ✅ Building insured post-construction; school incorporated under Guatemalan law and holds title to the land and building
- ✅ Instruction delivered in both Tz'utujil (local Mayan language) and Spanish, preserving indigenous culture

---

### Project Story
This project had a long runway: needs assessment visits began in December 2018, architectural plans were submitted to and approved by the Guatemalan Ministry of Education by mid-2019, and the grant was approved by Rotary International in late 2019 — but construction did not begin until March 2021, over a year behind the originally planned January 2020 start (likely delayed in part by the COVID-19 pandemic, though this is not explicitly stated in the documents). Once construction began, the structural phases (foundations, walls, slab, roof) proceeded efficiently and were completed by June 2021; finishing work, windows, doors, bathrooms, electrical, playground, and painting continued through November 2021. The scope shifted slightly from the original application: the school had planned to serve through 1st grade but ultimately focused on preschool and kindergarten (ages 3–5), serving the same number of children with the same facilities. Financially, the project came in under budget overall (894,035 GTQ spent of 908,091 GTQ budgeted), though training costs significantly exceeded the original estimate (20,299 GTQ spent vs. 5,202 GTQ budgeted) and operations ran slightly over, offset by meaningful savings on supplies and project management. The school opened fully functional, is self-sufficient, and PEG Partners retains an ongoing financial support role — ensuring the institution that has served this Mayan village for over fourteen years now has a permanent, community-owned home.

---

### Notes
- The school was originally founded by a local Mayan man and has been supported by PEG Partners for all 14+ years of its operation — an unusually deep and sustained NGO–community partnership that preceded and outlasted the Rotary grant.
- The school building and all grant-purchased assets are owned by the Escuela Comunitaria David LaMotte (a Guatemalan incorporated association), not by any Rotary entity, in compliance with Foundation guidelines.
- Future aspiration stated by teachers and community: expand the school to serve higher grade levels.
- Post-construction building insurance was purchased as a contingency expense — a practical and forward-thinking addition not included in the original budget.

title: SANIK-YA/ CHITULUL GUATEMALA WATER PROJECT
description:
The community is two villages, Sanik-Ya and Chitulul, near San Lucas Toliman. The area's water source is Lake
Atitlan, which in 2009 had the dubious distinction of being named by the Gloal Nature Fund as 'threatened lake
of the year'. The Mayan Villagers through COCODE began looking for who could assist them with their greatest
need which is to provide safe water for drinking and sanitation to their new community. The objectives of this
project are to separately meter 160 homes, provide safely filtered water to those homes, assist COCODE and
the local municipality with the installation of a water filtration system and holding tanks (one holding, one
sedimentation) provide initial training in system maintenance, and assist the village in setting up a billing
surcharge system to add to the San Lucas Toliman municipal distribution of water that is chlorinated but not
filtered for pathogens. A secondary goal will be to initially train locals on sanitation and use of safe water and
have the local doctor and hospital adopt a measurement system to identify whether or not there are measurable
health benefits from safe water. Dr. Paul Wise of Stanford (CV uploaded under Participants) developed the
measurement system, and Dr. Rafael Tun of Hospital Obras Sociales Monsignor Gregorio Schaffer (hereinafter
'local hospital' and 'Dr. Tun'). will measure results. The local E Club of Atitlan is the Host Rotary and
COCODE reached out to them through a local Mayan non-profit, Agua del Pueblo, of which one of their
Rotary Club members, Bruce Clemens, has volunteered for in doing Mayan water projects in Guatemala since
1972.
narrative:
## GG1529575 — Sanik-Ya / Chitulul Guatemala Water Project
*Global Grant · 2015–2018 · Sanik-Ya and Chitulul villages, near San Lucas Toliman, Lake Atitlán, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project delivered a safe drinking water system to two newly formed Mayan villages near Lake Atitlán, Guatemala. The core work included installation of a SunSpring solar-powered filtration system, a 150,000-liter storage tank, a pump and generator, PVC distribution piping, and 160 individual household water meters. A billing surcharge structure was established to fund long-term maintenance, and a health monitoring program was designed to measure measurable improvements in community health outcomes over time.

---

### Community Served
Approximately 960–1,000 residents across 160 Mayan families in the villages of Sanik-Ya and Chitulul, who had formed a new community since 2009 and lacked access to filtered, pathogen-safe drinking water despite proximity to the heavily polluted Lake Atitlán.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $89,577 |
| Rotary Foundation World Fund match | $39,856 |
| District Designated Fund (District 5170) | $29,991 |
| E-Club of Lake Atitlán–Panajachel (cash) | $9,980 |
| Non-Rotarian contributions (matched by TRF) | $9,750 |
| **Total actual expenditures** | **GTQ 649,114 (~$85,185)** |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Santa Cruz (District 5170) — International sponsor *(North America)*
- E-Club of Lake Atitlán–Panajachel (District 4250) — Host sponsor *(Guatemala)*
- Santa Cruz Sunrise Rotary Club (District 5170) *(North America)*
- San Lorenzo Valley / Felton Rotary Club *(North America)*
- Fremont Rotary Club *(North America)*
- Pleasanton North Rotary Club *(North America)*
- Freedom Rotary Club *(North America)*
- Sunnyvale Sunrise Rotary Club *(North America)*
- Sunnyvale Rotary Club *(North America)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- Asociación Pro Agua del Pueblo (ADP), Quetzaltenango, Guatemala — designed the system, supervised construction, and conducted the majority of training
- COCODE (Consejo Comunitario de Desarrollo) — elected local governing body that led community planning and will own and operate the system
- Hospital Obras Sociales Monsignor Gregorio Schaffer — local hospital partner for health outcome monitoring
- H2Open Doors — installer of the SunSpring filtration system
- Municipality of San Lucas Toliman — coordinated municipal water supply connection

---

### Key Personnel
- **Larry Wallerstein** — International primary contact, Rotary Club of Santa Cruz
- **Dwight Mara-Poage** — Host primary contact, E-Club of Lake Atitlán
- **Dr. Bruce Clemens (PhD)** — Member, E-Club of Lake Atitlán; Director, Agua del Pueblo; volunteer traveler; project coordinator on the ground; has worked in Guatemalan WASH projects since 1972
- **Dr. Paul Wise** — Stanford University; developed the health measurement protocol; volunteer traveler
- **Dr. Rafael Tun** — Local hospital physician; responsible for collecting and reporting health outcome data
- **Jim Zenner** — International committee, Santa Cruz; general project manager, secured donations, arranged SunSpring procurement
- **Joe Wakely** — Host club; financial management lynchpin; collected and reviewed all receipts and managed fund disbursements to ADP
- **William Boegel** — Host committee secondary contact; co-led the project effort
- **Sharon Smart** — Host committee secondary contact
- **Karen Gossling** — Incoming President, Santa Cruz Sunrise; joined installation team

---

### Outcomes
- **SunSpring solar-powered filtration system** installed and operational
- **150,000-liter storage tank** constructed and operational
- **160 individual household water meters** purchased, shipped, and installed
- **PVC distribution piping** installed connecting the system to homes
- **Pump and generator** procured and installed
- **1,000 people** reported as direct beneficiaries (up from the originally projected ~600)
- **14,000+ person-days of community labor** contributed by beneficiary families
- **1 functioning COCODE governance committee** in place to manage and maintain the system
- **1 community tariff/usage fee structure** established to fund ongoing maintenance and create a sinking fund for future repairs
- **Health monitoring program** initiated, tracking illness episodes, child growth, parasitic load in feces, and reported deaths or illnesses (surveys conducted annually with ~50 beneficiaries)
- **Community and local municipality took the lead** in design and construction — project exceeded stated objectives and was completed approximately six months ahead of schedule
- Ownership of all assets transferred to the community through COCODE

---

### Project Story
This project had an unusually strong foundation in community self-determination: the 160 Mayan families had already been building their community since 2009, laying roads, arranging municipal water connections, and forming their own governance body before Rotary was ever involved — they simply needed help with the filtration and metering components they could not otherwise afford. The project came in under budget (GTQ 649,114 spent against a GTQ 682,574 budget) and was completed roughly six months ahead of the December 2016 deadline, a noteworthy result for infrastructure work in a remote Guatemalan setting. The scope did evolve: a budget line item for sustainability upgrades was added mid-project (additional submersible pump, security for the SunSpring unit, strengthened pump house, meters for households added since project inception), reflecting real-world conditions on the ground. The monitoring and evaluation component — designed by Stanford's Dr. Paul Wise and administered locally by Dr. Rafael Tun — ran through January 2018, giving the project an unusually rigorous health-impact measurement dimension rare for a grant of this size. Long-term sustainability was addressed through the COCODE-administered tariff system, and ADP's ongoing presence in Guatemala provided continuity of technical oversight well beyond the grant period.

---

### Notes
- Lake Atitlán was named the Global Nature Fund's "threatened lake of the year" in 2009 — the same year this community was founded — underscoring both the environmental context and the urgency of providing filtered (not merely chlorinated) water.
- The SunSpring membrane module was selected partly for its extremely low maintenance profile: the main membrane filter requires no replacement for 10–15 years, and subsidiary filters need only periodic cleaning with water.
- The final report notes no challenges in financial management and no unexpected variances, an uncommon outcome for international infrastructure grants of this complexity.
- Seven Rotary clubs beyond the two sponsors contributed participation, reflecting broad District 5170 engagement with this project.
