narrative:
## GG2459764 — Santa Cruz La Laguna After School Tutoring Program

*Global Grant · 2024–2025 · Santa Cruz La Laguna, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project established a comprehensive after-school tutoring program for 90 children (preschool to Grade 6) in Santa Cruz La Laguna, pairing professional teacher training with hands-on learning resources and a solar power installation to support a reliable learning environment. Six teachers and one social worker received intensive training delivered by education specialists Dos Pajaritas, focused on critical thinking, lesson planning, student assessment, and parental involvement. A children's learning space was equipped with educational manipulatives, and 90 mothers were engaged through regular educational workshops.

### Community Served
Santa Cruz La Laguna is a small, remote Tz'utujil Maya community on Lake Atitlán where children were assessed as two to three grade levels behind peers in comparable Guatemalan communities, public school instruction relied on rote learning, and pandemic closures had wiped out nearly three years of schooling for most students.

---

### Budget

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $39,980 USD |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $11,420 USD |
| Club cash contribution (Rotary Club of Wiarton) | $14,280 USD |
| District 6330 DDF contribution | $14,280 USD |
| Other sources | Not stated |

*Exchange rate used: 1 USD = 7.81 GTQ (set 02/02/2024). Total budget in local currency: GTQ 312,248.*

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Wiarton, District 6330 *(International sponsor — Ontario, Canada)*
- Rotary e-Club of Lake Atitlán–Panajachel, District 4250 *(Host sponsor — Guatemala)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Amigos de Santa Cruz la Laguna** — local NGO with 25+ years of community presence; provided facility, employed teachers, managed finances, monitored outcomes, and will sustain the program post-grant
- **Dos Pajaritas** (Santiago Atitlán) — educational consultancy; designed the tutoring curriculum and delivered all teacher training; co-founders include an international early education expert and a registered psychologist

---

### Key Personnel

| Name | Role |
|------|------|
| Patricia Cavan | International Primary Contact; retired school principal, Rotary Club of Wiarton |
| Armand Boissy | Host Primary Contact; Treasurer, Rotary e-Club Lake Atitlán |
| Glenda Jamieson | International Secondary Contact, Wiarton |
| Robert Carson | International Secondary Contact, Wiarton |
| Duncan Aitken | Host Secondary Contact, Lake Atitlán |
| Mayra Tobías | Host Secondary Contact, Lake Atitlán (lawyer; disclosed advisory relationship with Amigos) |
| Jessie Cohn | Executive Director, Amigos de Santa Cruz la Laguna |

---

### Outcomes

- **90 children** enrolled in the after-school tutoring program (preschool–Grade 6); attendance and enrolment confirmed in progress report
- **6 teachers and 1 social worker** received professional development training covering lesson planning, critical thinking pedagogy, student assessment, and parental engagement
- **Tutoring program design** completed by Dos Pajaritas, aligned with Guatemala Ministry of Education curriculum
- **Educational manipulatives** purchased (replacing originally planned library books, with Foundation approval March 2025) to support hands-on, experiential learning in math and science
- **Library furniture** purchased and installed (GTQ 16,982)
- **Solar power system** installation initiated (in progress as of report date, June–July 2025)
- **90 mothers** engaged in educational workshops as a condition of their children's participation
- Student reading assessments conducted at 6-month and 12-month intervals; results documented and submitted

---

### Project Story

The project launched with strong community foundations — Amigos de Santa Cruz had already demonstrated a 24% GPA increase in high school students through evening tutoring, and 90% of their after-school reading program participants advanced to middle school versus 50% in the general population. Implementation began in July 2024 with planning meetings and teacher training proceeding on schedule, and the 90-student enrolment was completed by January 2025. The most significant scope change came when teachers, just months into training, requested that library book funds be redirected to educational manipulatives — a pedagogically sound shift they observed firsthand as students engaged more deeply with tactile learning tools, and one formally approved by the Foundation. The solar power installation, however, was delayed by unforeseen circumstances and remained in progress at the time of the progress report (June–July 2025), meaning the project extended beyond its original April 2025 end date. Financial management was transparent throughout, with Amigos submitting monthly itemized expense reports and invoices to Armand Boissy, who managed disbursements and bank reconciliation; of the GTQ 312,248 budget, GTQ 195,029 had been expended at progress report stage, with solar equipment costs (budgeted at GTQ ~87,000) yet to be drawn down.

---

### Notes

- The conflict of interest involving Mayra Tobías (advisory relationship with Amigos de Santa Cruz) was proactively disclosed in the application and accepted by the Foundation.
- This grant builds directly on a prior Global Grant between the same two Rotary clubs, demonstrating an ongoing partnership rather than a one-off project.
- Sustainability is built in: Amigos owns the building, will absorb ongoing teacher salaries within its "Education and Youth Program" budget, and CECAP (its vocational training centre) offers solar maintenance courses — training community members to service the new system.
- The Teacher Assistant position was intentionally funded for one year only, as a structured transition support while teachers built confidence implementing new methods independently.

title: Amigos de Santa Cruz 3 / Economic Dev
description: To contribute to the economic development opportunities for Santa Cruz through training courses and workshops. By the end of 2025, 35 owners of 10 different businesses will have acquired the skills they need to develop, flourish and compete in the local market
narrative:
## GG2567164 — Economic Development Santa Cruz, Guatemala
*Global Grant · 2025 · Santa Cruz la Laguna, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project delivered entrepreneurship training, vocational workshops, branding support, and seed capital to indigenous small-business owners in Santa Cruz la Laguna, Guatemala. Working through the local NGO Amigos de Santa Cruz, the grant funded courses in business management, digital marketing, accounting, and culinary and cultural product development. A seed capital contest provided startup funding to five winning entrepreneurs following completion of their business plans.

---

### Community Served
Indigenous entrepreneurs — prioritising women — in the lakeside municipality of Santa Cruz la Laguna, Sololá, Guatemala, a community with limited access to business training, capital, and markets.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant | $55,000 USD (GTQ 425,695) |
| Rotary Foundation World Fund | $15,600 USD |
| District 6330 DDF contribution | $19,700 USD |
| Rotary Club of Watford (cash) | $19,700 USD |

---

### Partner Organizations
**Rotary Clubs:**
- Rotary Club of Watford, District 6330 — International Sponsor *(UK)*
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan, District 4250 — Host Sponsor *(Guatemala)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations:**
- **Amigos de Santa Cruz** (www.amigosdesantacruz.org) — Cooperating organization; 25-year track record in Santa Cruz; responsible for instructor selection, community assessment, participant selection, financial reporting, and ongoing post-grant support.

---

### Key Personnel
- **Trudy Morris** — International Primary Contact, Rotary Club of Watford
- **Wayne Morris** — Secondary Contact International, Rotary Club of Watford; made site visit to Guatemala, February 2025
- **Armand Boissy** — Host Primary Contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan; project financial manager
- **Jaime Marroquin Mendoza** — Secondary Contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan
- **Edward Seeger** — Secondary Contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan
- **Maggie Connell** — Secondary Contact International, Rotary Club of Watford
- **Kevin Vasques** — Local instructor/consultant; designed courses and implemented business management support program
- **Edin Marroquin** — English for Customer Service instructor
- **Santos Canel** — Food workshop instructor
- **Maria Emig** — Rotary Foundation officer who approved budget reallocation (October 2025)

---

### Outcomes
- **18 entrepreneurs** directly supported through business management, digital marketing, accounting, and branding courses
- **19 individuals** trained across all programs (tested)
- **14 businesses** directly supported
- **10 entrepreneurs** received complete brand identity manuals with professional logo designs
- **3 vocational workshops** completed: Oyster Mushroom Production, Birdwatching/Aviturismo (cultural tourism), and Culinary Program (international, vegetarian, vegan, and special-diet menus)
- **15 individuals** benefited from product/service improvement training programs
- **5 entrepreneurs** entered the seed capital contest; seed capital distribution was in progress at time of report
- **Business plans and investment plans** completed for at least 10 new enterprises
- **English curriculum redesigned** (November–December 2025) to align with established language-teaching methodology tailored to customer service
- **Digital campaign** generated 10 client contacts to date (target: 100 — not yet met at time of report)
- **22 people** identified as direct project beneficiaries in progress report

---

### Project Story
The project launched on schedule in January 2025 and moved quickly through its core training activities, with business management, digital marketing, accounting, and branding courses all completed by mid-2025. However, implementation feedback prompted meaningful scope adjustments: the carpentry workshop was cancelled due to lack of participation, and those funds (~$4,000) were reallocated — with Foundation approval — toward digital visibility, promotional activities, organizational collaboration workshops, and a strengthened English curriculum. The vocational training pivot proved productive, with mushroom cultivation and culinary workshops generating tangible new product lines for participants. At the time of the progress report (December 2025), the project was not fully complete: seed capital distribution was still in progress, the English for Customer Service course had not yet begun delivery (only curriculum redesign was done), and the digital marketing campaign was well short of its 100-contact target. The Amigos de Santa Cruz committed to continuing business mentorship and technical support for entrepreneurs after the grant concluded, providing a meaningful sustainability handoff.

---

### Notes
- The carpentry workshop cancellation was handled transparently and the reallocation was formally approved — a good example of adaptive management within grant guidelines.
- The initial GTQ deposit conversion came in GTQ 4,551 short of budget forecast due to exchange rate movement; the Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan covered the bank's minimum deposit requirement from club funds.
- The progress report was filed December 2025 as a mid-project report; a final report covering seed capital outcomes and English course delivery will be required to close the grant.
- Women-owned indigenous businesses were explicitly prioritised throughout all three program streams.

title: Hospitalito Atitlan - Mothers and Infants
description:
This project seeks to improve maternal and neonatal health outcomes for Tz'utujil Maya women in Santiago
 Atitlán, Guatemala, by enhancing local healthcare infrastructure and expanding community-based prenatal
 support. oject purchases neo-natal equipment for HA and the rural clinics it operates.  It provides funding for 16 Tsutujil-speaking "Promotadoras".
narrative:
## GG2570080 — Saving Mothers and Babies in the Rural Guatemalan Highlands
*Global Grant · 2025–2026 · Santiago Atitlán, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project upgraded Hospitalito Atitlán's medical infrastructure with new ultrasound machines, neonatal warming and resuscitation tables, and advanced laboratory equipment to enable local prenatal screenings and emergency obstetrical care. Simultaneously, 16 community-based Health Promoters (Promotoras) were trained and equipped to deliver culturally sensitive prenatal education in the Tz'utujil Maya language, conducting home visits and helping families develop emergency birth plans. A prenatal educational video in Tz'utujil and illustrated booklets were also produced to extend health literacy across the broader community.

---

### Community Served
Tz'utujil Maya women and families in Santiago Atitlán, Sololá, Guatemala (population ~60,000–64,000), a community with documented maternal and infant deaths and significant barriers — cultural, geographic, and economic — to accessing emergency obstetrical care.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $130,392 |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $38,662 |
| District Designated Funds (DDF) — all districts | $48,508 |
| Club cash contributions | $43,755 |
| Total funding secured | $130,925 |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs & Districts:**
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán, District 4250 *(Host — Guatemala)*
- Rotary Club of Truckee, District 5190 *(International sponsor — California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Truckee Sunrise, District 5190 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Oakville, District 5190 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Reno Central, District 5190 *(Nevada, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Tahoe-Incline, District 5190 *(Nevada/California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Corral de Tierra, District 5190 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Oakland, District 5190 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Fort Collins-Breakfast, District 5440 *(Colorado, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Greeley (Centennial), District 5440 *(Colorado, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Loveland, District 5440 *(Colorado, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Cupertino, District 5170 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Livermore Valley, District 5170 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Hanford, District 5230 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of North Fresno, District 5230 *(California, USA)*
- Rotary Club of Milton, District 7080 *(Ontario, Canada)*
- Rotary Club of Kitchener, District 7080 *(Ontario, Canada)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations:**
- Asociación Pro Salud Educación y Desarrollo K'aslimaal / Hospitalito Atitlán — primary cooperating organization and equipment owner
- Department of Health / MSPAS District 4, Centro de Atención Permanente, Santiago Atitlán — government health partner for outreach coordination and Promotora reporting

---

### Key Personnel
- **Michael Rosen** — Host primary contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán, District 4250
- **Jon Gresley** — International primary contact, Rotary Club of Truckee, District 5190
- **Jaime Marroquin Mendoza** — Host secondary contact, RC Lake Atitlán
- **Candis Krummel** — Host secondary contact, RC Lake Atitlán
- **Janet Van Gelder** — International secondary contact, RC Truckee
- **Gudrun Dybdal** — International secondary contact, RC Truckee
- **Lyn Dickey** — Director of Development, Hospitalito Atitlán; grant monitoring and evaluation lead (25 years in Guatemala; $1M+ in prior grant management)
- **Dr. Manuel González Hi** — Executive Director, Hospitalito Atitlán; Tz'utujil Maya orthopedic surgeon, key bridge between traditional healers and western medicine

---

### Outcomes
*The Progress Report on file is a draft with no expenditures logged and no results entered — these remain intended outcomes.*

- Upgrade Hospitalito Atitlán with: Toshiba ultrasound, Butterfly IQ handheld ultrasound, 2× GE Panda Giraffe neonatal warming/resuscitation tables, fetal monitors, dopplers, full laboratory suite (centrifuge, autoclave, inverted microscope, laminar flow hood, incubator, automated blood culture processor, refrigeration, pipettes, and more)
- Provide comprehensive prenatal care to **500+ pregnant women** annually through hospital and four rural clinic sites
- Train **HA physicians and radiology/lab staff** on new ultrasound and laboratory equipment via contracted Guatemalan consultant
- Train and equip **16 Community Health Promoters (Promotoras)** in Tz'utujil-language prenatal education, home visiting, and emergency birth planning
- Train **Ministry of Health Promoters** in coordination with MSPAS District 4
- Produce a **prenatal educational video** in Tz'utujil Maya language; distribute **16 tablets** for community video demonstrations
- Fund **500 nurse outreach home visits** to pregnant women and families
- Expand laboratory capacity to include local testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea, reducing costly off-site transfers to Panajachel
- Track high-risk pregnancies using **REDCap electronic database**; share data quarterly with Ministry of Health

---

### Project Story
The application tells a compelling and well-documented story of need: in a Tz'utujil Maya community of 60,000 where five women died in childbirth in 2023–2024 and 50% of births occur at home with traditional attendants, Hospitalito Atitlán — the only provider of emergency C-sections in the region — was operating with aging and failing neonatal equipment dating to a 2004 donation container. The partnership between RC Lake Atitlán and RC Truckee was formed through a long personal relationship between club leaders and reinforced by multiple site visits, including a 2023 trip that led to a prior District 5190 equipment grant. The global grant was approved and authorized by all parties by early January 2025, with a project window of January 2025 through June 2026. However, the single progress report on file is an empty draft — no expenditures have been logged, no activities marked complete, and no outcome data entered — indicating that as of the report date, the project was either in very early stages or reporting had not yet been completed. Given the thorough groundwork laid in the application and Hospitalito Atitlán's strong track record managing major international grants, the infrastructure and community trust needed for success is clearly in place; the full story of implementation and impact remains to be written.

---

### Notes
- Hospitalito Atitlán has a documented history of successful large-scale grant management: World Diabetes Foundation ($610,000), IZUMI Foundation ($325,000), Adelphia Foundation ($150,000), and multiple prior Rotary grants over 15 years.
- In 2022, Lyn Dickey led construction of a four-clinic annex with blood bank and accredited auxiliary nursing school — completed on time and on budget at $400,000 — demonstrating strong institutional execution capacity.
- The project introduces a culturally significant innovation: training *comadrona* (traditional birth attendant) leaders to accompany women to hospital deliveries and perform traditional newborn ceremonies, directly integrating indigenous practice into western medical settings.
- Equipment will be owned by Asociación K'aslimaal / Hospitalito Atitlán upon project completion; no Rotary club or district will hold title.
- The grant mobilized **17 Rotary clubs across 6 districts** and **2 countries**, representing an unusually broad coalition for a single global grant.

title: Reforesting Santiago
description: This project restores ecosystems, provides families with sustainable source of firewood, imporoves access to clean water and strengthens community resilience.
narrative:
## GG2574529 — Reforesting the Tz'utujil Maya Mountains of Lake Atitlan
*Global Grant · 2025–2027 · Santiago Atitlan, Solola, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project aimed to reforest degraded land in the Tz'utujil Maya region of Lake Atitlan by planting 15,000 native trees integrated with food crops, using satellite technology and ARCGIS to monitor survival rates. In parallel, the project established a community-run native tree nursery managed by local women, who received training in tree propagation, agroecology, and small business management. Farmer workshops on sustainable reforestation and agroecological practices were included to build lasting community capacity.

---

### Community Served
The Tz'utujil Maya community of Santiago Atitlan, Solola, Guatemala — a population facing extreme poverty, chronic malnutrition, and rapid loss of the Rey Tepepul Nature Reserve (over 30% deforested) — with the project addressing linked crises of biodiversity loss, water scarcity, soil degradation, and food insecurity.

---

### Budget
| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant | $36,000 |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $8,800 |
| DDF contributions (Districts 5340 & 7780) | $11,000 |
| Cash from clubs (E-Club San Diego, Yarmouth, Lake Atitlan) | $16,200 |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- E-Club of San Diego Global — District 5340 (North America; international sponsor)
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan — District 4250 (Guatemala; host sponsor)
- Rotary Club of Yarmouth — District 7780 (North America; financial contributor)

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Wellkind Guatemala** — Santiago Atitlan; led ecological monitoring, tree survival tracking via satellite/ARCGIS, biodiversity assessments, and vendor relationships
- **Tuik Ruch Lew** (www.trlearth.org) — Santiago Atitlan; led farmer training evaluation, knowledge-retention surveys, and agroecological practice monitoring
- **CONAP** (National Council of Protected Areas) — institutional ally for alignment with national environmental policy and protection of the Rey Tepepul Nature Reserve
- Local community leaders and indigenous leaders of Santiago Atitlan — ongoing oversight and post-grant stewardship

---

### Key Personnel
- **Maureen Duncan** — International primary contact, E-Club of San Diego, District 5340
- **Michael Rosen** — Host primary contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan, District 4250
- **Edward Seeger** — Host secondary contact, Lake Atitlan
- **Mayra Tobías** — Host secondary contact, Lake Atitlan
- **Jaime Marroquin Mendoza** — Host secondary contact, Lake Atitlan
- **Amy Vegter, Amanda Wirtz Rosolowski, Gillian Semmer** — International secondary contacts, E-Club of San Diego
- **Michael Whitehurst** — DRFC, District 5340 (Point Loma, San Diego)
- **José Interiano Torre** — DRFC, District 4250 (Usula)

---

### Outcomes
*The progress report on file is a Draft with no expenditures recorded and no results entered — these remain intended outcomes.*

- Plant 15,000 native trees integrated with food crops across degraded mountain lands
- Establish and operationalize a women-managed community native tree nursery
- Train 100–499 farmers in reforestation techniques and agroecological practices
- Monitor tree survival rates quarterly using satellite imagery and ARCGIS technology
- Measure adoption of agroecological practices annually via direct observation
- Track economic development outcomes (500–999 beneficiaries projected) from nursery sales, local produce, handicrafts, and tourism
- Transfer ownership of tools (machetes, pruning shears) to trained participating farmers; transfer nursery ownership collectively to the women's cooperative
- Engage CONAP to align the project with protection of the Rey Tepepul Nature Reserve

---

### Project Story
The grant was approved and fully authorized by both primary contacts and all DRFC chairs by late July 2025, with a project window of August 2025 through September 2027. The partnership grew from a genuine site visit: the E-Club of San Diego traveled to Lake Atitlan in December 2024, meeting the host club, both NGO partners, and visiting the proposed planting and nursery sites firsthand — an unusual and commendable level of due diligence. The project deliberately avoided working with municipal reforestation programs due to documented local corruption concerns, instead building an independent, community-accountable structure. However, the only progress report on file is an unfiled draft showing zero expenditures against the full $36,000 budget and no results entered for any impact measure, suggesting the report was generated before project activities began or that reporting has not yet been completed. No conclusions about actual implementation, outcomes, or challenges can be drawn from available documentation.

---

### Notes
- The project's long-term funding model is notably self-sustaining in design: the women's nursery is intended to generate income through tree sales, vegetables, fruit, and artisan goods sold to tourists, reducing dependency on future grant cycles.
- The project explicitly chose not to use competitive bidding for vendors, justified by the specialized local context and the deliberate decision to source trees from the newly established women's greenhouse — a rationale worth documenting for future grant applications in similar contexts.
- Both cooperating NGOs (Wellkind and Tuik Ruch Lew) had previously completed other environment grants in the Lake Atitlan area, lending credibility to the implementation plan.

title: Water, Sanitation, Hygiene for Vista Hermosa, Phase I
description: The project will deliver a new well, pump, and household water distribution system to provide reliable potable water to families and a school in Vista Hermosa near Santa Lucía Utatlán, Guatemala.
narrative:
## GG2570516 — Water, Sanitation & Hygiene for Vista Hermosa, Guatemala (Phase 1)

*Global Grant · 2025–2026 · Vista Hermosa, Santa Lucía Utatlán, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project drilled and lined a well, installed a pump, and built a household water distribution system with individual meters to deliver clean, reliable potable water to a remote highland Guatemalan village for the first time. Alongside the infrastructure work, Agua del Pueblo provided WASH hygiene training in the community school and trained the existing village water committee in long-term system operation and maintenance. The project is Phase 1 of a two-phase initiative; Phase 2 (GG2578692, in draft at time of application) will add mini wastewater treatment to protect the water source and Lake Atitlán watershed.

---

### Community Served
Vista Hermosa is a mountainous village at 8,200 ft elevation, located 2.5 miles from the municipal capital of Santa Lucía Utatlán, with 146 residents in 41 households and 50 students at a regional school, all of whom previously depended on an intermittent, deteriorating water source requiring women to walk 3–4 hours daily to fetch water from a remote stream.

---

### Budget

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total project budget | $34,140 (GTQ 262,875) |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $4,890 |
| Club cash contributions | $20,750 |
| District Designated Funds (DDF) | $6,500 |
| TRF Donor Advised Fund (WASH-RAG) | $2,000 |

*Exchange rate: GTQ 7.7 = USD 1.00, set 20 October 2024.*

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Annapolis — District 7620 *(International sponsor, North America)*
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán — District 4250 *(Host sponsor, Guatemala)*
- Rotary Club of Baltimore — District 7620 *(North America)*
- Rotary Club of Metro Bethesda — District 7620 *(North America)*
- Rotary Club of Parole (Annapolis) — District 7620 *(North America)*
- Rotary Club of South Anne Arundel County (Edgewater) — District 7620 *(North America)*
- Rotary Club of Calgary Heritage Park — District 5370 *(Canada)*
- Rotary Club of Petaluma — District 5130 *(North America)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Asociación Pro Agua del Pueblo (AdP)** — Lead cooperating organization; 53-year history, 500+ rural WASH projects in Guatemala; responsible for engineering design, construction management, community organization, and hygiene training
- **Municipalidad de Santa Lucía Utatlán** — Legal oversight, skilled labor, and materials; previously partnered with RCLA on two global grants
- **Vista Hermosa Water Committee (VWC)** / **COCODE** — Village-level governance body; co-identified needs, purchased well site, secured rights-of-way, and will operate/maintain the system post-project
- **WASH Remedial Action Group (WASH-RAG)** — Contributed $2,000 via Donor Advised Fund; Bruce Clemens serves as Secretary of its Board

---

### Key Personnel

| Name | Role |
|------|------|
| **Lydia Hohman** | International primary contact, RC Annapolis, District 7620 |
| **Clinton White** | Host primary contact, RC Lake Atitlán, District 4250 |
| **Bruce Clemens** | Host committee, RC Lake Atitlán; co-founder of Agua del Pueblo (1972); disclosed conflict of interest — no remuneration since 1980 |
| **Duncan Aitken** | Host committee secondary contact, RC Lake Atitlán |
| **Luisa Gil, Debra McGhee, Philip Reynolds** | International committee secondary contacts, RC Annapolis |
| **Geetha Jayaram** | DRFC, District 7620 (authorized DRFC & DDF) |
| **José Interiano Torre** | DRFC, District 4250 (authorized DRFC) |
| **Michael Rosen** | Legal agreement signatory, RC Lake Atitlán |
| **Robert Young** | Legal agreement signatory, RC Annapolis |

---

### Outcomes

*Intended outcomes — no completion reports available at time of summary.*

- Potable water delivered to 41 households (146 residents) and 1 regional school (50 students)
- Individual water meters installed in each home to quantify usage and fund ongoing maintenance via a graduated tariff system
- Women freed from 3–4 hours of daily water-fetching, enabling income-generating activities
- Increased child safety by eliminating solo travel to remote water source
- WASH hygiene training delivered to school students and community families
- Village water committee trained in system operation and maintenance using the mWater digital monitoring platform
- Municipal Water Department engaged for ongoing system oversight
- Beneficiary contracts signed requiring unskilled labor contribution, rights-of-way, and monthly tariff payment

---

### Project Story

Only the grant application is available; no interim or final reports were included in the materials reviewed. The application reflects a well-developed partnership: the two clubs first connected at the 2017 RI Convention in Atlanta, deepened the relationship through community assessment visits, and formally committed to the Vista Hermosa project in September 2024. The community itself identified the need, purchased the well site, and secured rights-of-way before the grant was even submitted — a strong indicator of genuine local ownership. The conflict of interest involving Bruce Clemens (co-founder of the implementing partner AdP) was disclosed transparently and accepted by TRF, given that his financial relationship with AdP ended in 1980. The project is explicitly designed as Phase 1 of a two-phase initiative, with Phase 2 wastewater treatment (GG2578692) intended to protect both the new water source and the broader Lake Atitlán watershed.

---

### Notes

- This project is the **seventh community** served under RC Lake Atitlán's long-running **"Save the Lake" (StL) Program**, which aims to bring sustainable WASH services to all communities in the 400,000-person Lake Atitlán watershed.
- RCLA has previously completed **three global grants and two district grants** in partnership with Agua del Pueblo, providing a strong foundation of trust and operational experience.
- AdP's 50th-anniversary review of randomly selected past projects found **each one still operating with a functioning tariff system 40 years later** — a notable indicator of the model's long-term sustainability.
- The pump carries a **five-year guarantee**; the tariff is structured to fund pump replacement on a five-year cycle.
- Phase 2 wastewater treatment grant (GG2578692) was in draft status at the time of this application.

title: Water and Sanitation for Panimaquip, San Lucas Toliman

title: Protecting the Watershed of Lake Atitlan, Santa Lucia
description: This project will provide clean drinking water through household filters and sanitation systems while restoring degraded hillsides through the planting of thousands of native trees. The project also includes environmental education to promote long-term stewardship of water and natural resources. Together, these efforts aim to improve public health, reduce water contamination, and protect the watershed for future generations.
narrative:
## GG2684872 — Protecting the Watershed of Lake Atitlán

*Global Grant · 2026–2028 · Santa Lucía Utatlán, Sololá, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did

This project addressed the accelerating environmental degradation of the Lake Atitlán watershed by combining three integrated interventions: reforesting degraded hillsides in Santa Lucía Utatlán with 8,000 native trees, distributing water filters to 120 families, and installing a biodigester system to safely treat grey and black water before it drains into the lake basin. Training in climate resilience, ecological restoration, hygiene, and biodigester maintenance was delivered throughout. Data collection using ArcGIS, water quality testing, and community surveys was built into the project design to track and verify impact.

---

### Community Served

Indigenous farming families in Santa Lucía Utatlán, Sololá, Guatemala — a K'iche'-speaking highland community of over 550 direct beneficiaries whose water sources are increasingly polluted and whose hillsides have been severely deforested, threatening both livelihoods and the health of Lake Atitlán downstream.

---

### Budget

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $38,800 USD (GTQ 297,210) |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $11,000 |
| DDF contributions (Districts 6330 & 5440) | $13,750 |
| Club cash contributions (Wiarton, Fort Collins, Lake Atitlán) | $14,050 (incl. processing fees) |

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Wiarton, District 6330 *(international sponsor — Ontario, Canada)*
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán, District 4250 *(host sponsor — Guatemala)*
- Rotary Club of Fort Collins, District 5440 *(contributing club — Colorado, USA)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Wellkind** (Santiago, Guatemala) — reforestation lead; ArcGIS monitoring; community brigade coordination with COCODEs and indigenous leaders
- **Water4Life / Agua para La Vida** (Hopedale, MA, USA + Guatemalan Association) — filter distribution, biodigester installation, safe water education, and ongoing monitoring

---

### Key Personnel

- **Patricia Cavan** — Rotary Club of Wiarton; international primary contact; project champion who visited Guatemala in 2020, 2023, 2024, and 2025 to build the partnership
- **Michelle Fajkus** — Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán; host primary contact
- **Mayra Tobías** — Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán; host secondary contact; designated bank account signatory
- **Bruce Clemens** — Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán; host secondary contact
- **George Gordon & Donna Rudell** — Rotary Club of Wiarton; international secondary contacts
- **Shad Qudsi** — Treasurer, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán; also Board President of Wellkind (disclosed conflict of interest; removed from all financial management of the grant)
- **Jenna MacEwen** — Executive Director, Water4Life (USA); Board President, Agua para La Vida (Guatemala)
- **Duncan Atkens** — Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán; second designated bank account signatory
- **Sonja Glass** — DRFC Chair, District 6330 (Meaford, Ontario)
- **José Interiano Torre** — DRFC Chair, District 4250

---

### Outcomes

*Intended outcomes — no reports available at time of application approval.*

- **Reforestation:** 8,000 native trees planted on degraded hillsides; community brigades trained in site preparation, planting, post-planting care, and survival monitoring via ArcGIS geo-photography
- **Clean water access:** 120 filters distributed to selected households (550+ direct beneficiaries); families trained in filter use, cleaning, maintenance, and water quality testing
- **Wastewater treatment:** Biodigester system designed, installed, and validated by environmental engineer; treats grey and black water before discharge into Parracana River / Lake Atitlán basin
- **Education & training:** Four formal training programs delivered covering climate change awareness, tree planting skills, safe water practices, and biodigester operation/maintenance
- **Monitoring:** Monthly water quality testing (Year 1); bi-annual testing (Year 2); household logbooks maintained; quarterly ArcGIS field documentation; baseline and follow-up surveys administered
- **Community capacity:** COCODEs, indigenous leaders, women's groups, and local social workers trained as ongoing stewards; small community tax established to fund biodigester maintenance post-grant
- **Indirect beneficiaries:** 80 families (425 people) near the Parracana River reached through environmental education; hundreds more supported through improved watershed health

---

### Project Story

Only the approved grant application is available — no progress or final reports were accessible for this summary. What the application reveals, however, is a project built on an unusually deep foundation: a five-year relationship between Patricia Cavan and the Rotary Club of Lake Atitlán (beginning with a 2020 club visit, interrupted by the pandemic, and rekindled through annual returns to the region), and a prior successful Global Grant between the Wiarton and Lake Atitlán clubs that established trust and working procedures. The partnership with Wellkind and Water4Life grew organically from a February 2025 site visit, and both NGOs had existing, trusted relationships with K'iche'-speaking community leaders — a critical asset in a region where cultural appropriateness and local ownership have historically determined whether water and reforestation projects succeed or fail. One notable governance tension was disclosed and managed proactively: Shad Qudsi's dual role as club treasurer and Wellkind board president was flagged, and financial signatory authority was assigned entirely to other Rotarians. The project is scheduled to run February 2026 through February 2028; outcomes remain to be confirmed through reporting.

---

### Notes

- This is identified in club records as "GG2" for Santa Lucía, suggesting at least one prior Global Grant in the same region; the Wellkind quote document references "Paraiso," indicating Wellkind had previously worked with the club in a different community.
- The disclosed conflict of interest involving Shad Qudsi (Wellkind board president / club treasurer) was handled transparently and appears to have satisfied Rotary Foundation reviewers — the grant was approved.
- Water4Life's Guatemalan funds flow through their legally separate local association, *Agua para La Vida*, remaining in-country throughout the grant period.
- The project was authorized by all parties between November 18 and December 2, 2025.

title: Agroecological Vegetable Production in Santa Cruz La Laguna
description: The project promotes sustainable economic development through agroecological vegetable production and commercialization in Santa Cruz La Laguna, Guatemala, focusing on highly vulnerable upper communities such as Chuitzanchaj.  It provides training in business management, collective organization, productivity, post-harvest processing, and the creation of a local market to improve incomes and nutrition.  The project directly benefits 15 indigenous farming families (prioritizing women) and indirectly supports about 850 community members
narrative:
## GG2684482 — Agroecological Vegetable Production, Santa Cruz La Laguna

*Global Grant · 2026–2028 · Chuitzanchaj, Santa Cruz La Laguna, Solola, Guatemala*

---

### What We Did
This project promotes sustainable economic development by training 15 indigenous farming families in agroecological vegetable production, business management, marketing, post-harvest processing, and cooperative organization. The goal is to increase family incomes, restore degraded soils, and improve nutritional sovereignty in one of the most economically vulnerable communities in the Lake Atitlán region. The project culminates in the legal formalization of a farming cooperative and the establishment of a local market channel.

---

### Community Served
The 850 inhabitants of Chuitzanchaj, an upper highland village in Santa Cruz La Laguna, Guatemala, where 76% chronic malnutrition, 95% poverty rates, and extractive intermediary-dominated agriculture combine to create severe food insecurity and economic marginalization among indigenous Kaq'chikel-speaking families.

---

### Budget

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Total grant budget | $45,000 USD (GTQ 354,615) |
| Rotary Foundation (World Fund match) | $12,000 USD |
| Club cash contribution (Mildmay RC) | $18,000 USD |
| District Designated Funds (District 6330) | $15,000 USD |

*Exchange rate: GTQ 7.88 per USD, set 17 September 2025.*

---

### Partner Organizations

**Rotary Clubs**
- Rotary Club of Mildmay, District 6330 — International Sponsor *(Canada — North American)*
- Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan, District 4250 — Host Sponsor *(Guatemala)*

**NGOs & Local Organizations**
- **Amigos de Santa Cruz la Laguna** — Lead implementing organization; 25+ years in community; delivers business management, marketing, administration, organizational strengthening, and post-training follow-up; monitors and evaluates all outcomes
- **CEDRACC** (Centro de Educación para el Desarrollo Rural y Adaptación al Cambio Climático) — Technical partner; conducts agricultural assessment; delivers agroecological training using a Train-the-Trainer model; provides seeds, equipment, and ongoing technical support

---

### Key Personnel

- **Erin Wettlaufer** — International Primary Contact, Rotary Club of Mildmay
- **Shad Qudsi** — Host Primary Contact, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan
- **Dawn Kennedy** — Rotarian DGN, District 6330; co-initiated project during February 2025 site visit
- **Patricia Cavan** — DISC 6330; co-initiated project; planning February 2026 site visit
- **Jesse Cohn** — Executive Director, Amigos de Santa Cruz la Laguna
- **Nancy McCoy & Michelle Fajkus** — Secondary Contacts, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan
- **Julia Gutscher** — Secondary Contact International, Rotary Club of Mildmay
- **Kevin Vasquez** — Market study design and sales strategy
- **Sonja Glass** — DRFC, District 6330 (authorized)
- **José Interiano Torre** — DRFC, District 4250 (authorized)
- **Michael Rosen** — Legal agreement signatory, Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan

---

### Outcomes

*Intended outcomes — no final reports available at time of writing.*

- Train 15 indigenous farming families (with priority given to women) in five curriculum areas: business planning, commercialization/marketing, basic administration, organizational strengthening, and agroecological production
- Provide vegetable seeds, organic fertilizer, pest control products, agricultural tools, and irrigation equipment to participant families
- Deliver post-harvest processing training and equipment; introduce packaging and quality control systems
- Legally formalize a farming cooperative with governance structures for independent, post-grant operation
- Establish local market connections; target 60% of harvest sold through formal channels
- Achieve average monthly family income from vegetable sales of ≥ Q1,000/month
- Achieve ≥ 25% gross profit margin on production
- Achieve ≥ 75% of families reporting improved ability to cover basic expenses (food, schooling, health)
- Benefit 850 indirect community members in Chuitzanchaj
- Host a Launch Fair to introduce the cooperative to the local market

---

### Project Story

Only the approved grant application is available; the project is scheduled to run from January 2026 through January 2028, so implementation had not yet begun at the time this summary was prepared. The project originated from a February 2025 field visit by Rotarians Dawn Kennedy and Patricia Cavan, who observed stark economic disparities between lakeside tourist-economy villages and the more isolated upper community of Chuitzanchaj. Community consultation — conducted through Amigos staff fluent in Kaq'chikel — revealed that residents wanted not just improved agricultural practices but genuine economic independence, free from profit-extracting intermediaries; this shaped the strong business and cooperative-formation components of the design. The partnership with CEDRACC, which already operates a model sustainable-agriculture school nearby, was added during program development to strengthen the technical credibility of the agroecology curriculum. How the project unfolds in practice — whether timelines hold, whether the cooperative achieves legal formalization, and whether income targets are met — will be an important story for future reporting.

---

### Notes

- The project uses a **Train-the-Trainer model** through CEDRACC to build lasting local agricultural expertise within the cooperative beyond the grant period.
- Both Amigos de Santa Cruz and CEDRACC commit to providing **post-grant business and technical support**, a meaningful sustainability provision.
- All equipment and supplies will be **owned by individual participant businesses**; items from unsuccessful participants revert to Amigos for redistribution.
- The Rotary Club of Lake Atitlan has a **prior successful partnership history** with Amigos de Santa Cruz, lending confidence to the implementation model.
- Monitoring and evaluation will be conducted by **Amigos de Santa Cruz** using surveys, direct observation, and grant records on a quarterly and semi-annual basis.
